speaker: interviewee
text: Coming back, backing up a little bit, like I said, I knew those two addresses. So I wrote a letter to Israel. And I wrote a letter to my cousins and my brother in, in America. And they both wanted us-- me to come over there. At that time I wasn't married. And I proposed to Johanna. And she said that she would marry me if I come to America. So America it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made my decision. And we got married in, in-- May the 8th, 1946. And, and we applied for the papers previously. And my relatives in America said, why do you want to bring your wife? We have plenty of girls in America. You don't need to bring your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said to them, if I can't bring Johanna, I'm not coming either. So they agreed to that. And they made, made me the papers. And then we, we went-- we got married-- like I said, after we got married, there was just us. And then Johanna's brother and his wife were the witnesses. And we didn't even have a glass to break, you know. We didn't know-- we were just a couple kids. We didn't know the ritual.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came here on, on the Army transport ship, I think we, we left about-- we arrived on June the 22nd or 23rd, one of those. And we came to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the Hotel Marseilles in New York. We, we were-- you know, we were quartered there, you know. They gave us a room. And then our-- some of our cousins came to see us there. And they gave us $5 each, which was like a fortune for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So today is 49 years since you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: 49 years since we arrived, this month exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today. Yes, that's right--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's June--

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes, correct. We should observe it. Ah, we'll go out for dinner. [LAUGHS] Anyway, so when we came here, we stayed with my cousin for awhile. Well, first, we were New York for about a week. Then we stopped over in Denver. We had some cousins in Denver. So we stayed in Denver about 10 days, and-- you know, just to meet the relatives and see everybody. I'll never forget, they had a barbecue. And they had steaks. And the steaks were, like, rare-- medium rare. And we wouldn't touch it. And they couldn't believe how we could not eat it, you know. And of course our clothing was all-- completely out of touch in America, what we had. But anyway, when we came-- and then we came-- next stop was Los Angeles. We stayed with some cousins here. And frankly, they couldn't wait for us to go to work, you know. Unfortunately, they were not the type of cousins that would nurture you, you know. They thought they've done their responsibility by bringing us-- by helping us to come here. Of course, we would have come here anyway through the UNRRA even if they wouldn't because UNRRA brought people over here, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to go to work not speaking the language. It was very hard. I spoke a few words, but not enough, really, to communicate. So I just went.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I took a streetcar. I remember we lived in the neighborhood of Santa Monica Boulevard and Beverly Glen. And I took-- there was a red streetcar. And I took the streetcar as far as it went. And it stopped in Hollywood area in the industrial section.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just knocked on the doors and asked for a job. And that's how I got a job-- my first job. It was, like, working in a, a company that made costume jewelry doing assembly work, you know. That was my first job. And then I worked-- and Johanna, well, Johanna told you her story, right? So I don't have to repeat what she did.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we-- my second job was working for a company that made stoves here in this-- and I worked at a punch press putting metal into the punch press and then letting the punch press form the metal. Until I saw one person lost four fingers on his hand, so I quit. But the hard part was getting a place to live here because there were very few apartments because of the shortage due to the GIs coming back from the war. So we went to-- we had a-- very distant-- very distant relative who owned a building-- a triplex in Long Beach in Belmont Shore. So he let us have a small-- what do they call it-- a bachelor, a single apartment, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Long Beach. And the only job I could get was as a busboy in a restaurant. And then I graduated to be a waiter. But I had to be away from home constantly being a waiter. I had to work Saturday and Sunday and Friday night, you know. So we decided to quit that. And I-- we came back to Los Angeles. And I got a job at Mattel Toys as a mechanic. Mattel was owned by my cousins, Ruth and Elliot Hendler, you know. So they gave me the job. I got a job for a dollar an hour, which wasn't much money, really, even in those-- even in-- that was 1950, like. No, it was '48, actually, 1948. So I worked there. And as it worked out, I worked my way up to-- after-- I became a machinist and a model maker. And from model maker, I became a technician.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started going to college evenings. And take-- I took several courses in physics and manufacturing processes and mathematics-- algebra and statistics. And I worked myself up to being an engineer. And I was a test quality audit engineer. Then I was a-- they call it a reliability and product safety. And that's what I did until I retired, basically, you know. And-- but the way we got ahead in America, which is not through, basically, being on the job. Because that wasn't enough, really, to get ahead. It was just to make ends meet, like we say. But we-- in 1952, we borrowed, borrowed money from my cousins, the Hendlers, and from a couple of friends, two of our friends, and we bought our first house. Because that's what we strived for, to have a house. And then-- and we saved a little money. And we, we bought a house-- we, we bought some-- an old house in Culver City for, like, $8,000, which was a wreck. And we patched it and painted it. And I put new windows in. I put a new roof on it. And we sold it and made a couple thousand dollars. And from that, we bought another house. And that's how we gradually worked our way up, you know, until we could buy some income-producing property, you know, which basically helped us to achieve the status of living that we have now, thanks God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph, do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- I have a son Allan, who was born in April, 1948. So he's now 47. He is an attorney. And I have one son Michael, who is 43. And he's a, a repairman. He does plumbing, electrical, carpentry work. And he does quite well. And I have two grandchildren. One is Daniel, who is-- he's 12, and Derek, who is 10. Thanks God for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose children are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Allan's. My youngest son is not married yet. He is a bachelor. And, like I said, we had a very hard time with our grandson Daniel, who was born very much premature. Did Johanna tell you? Yeah? No? Yeah, he almost died. He only weighed, like, two and a half pounds. And then after awhile, he only weighed 2 pounds. And they had to operate on him, perform a colostomy. But thanks God that he survived. And he's OK. So we're grateful for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think one reason I survived is because I was young and strong-- healthy. And another thing is that I was lucky, you know. But, you know, it's-- a lot of people who were young and strong died, too, of illnesses and being shot or being beat to death, you know. So I guess it's a combination of being in a good physical condition at the right time and also being lucky that I was sent to, to a place where I could work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, I think, to a great measure-- at the right time, that I had the courage to speak to the Lageralteste, who was the Lagerfuhrer, you know? And-- because when I knew that I was going to die, and I had enough courage to speak up-- and consequently being transferred to a better place of work. So there's many aspects in it. And like they say in Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH], right? So that's what it is. Because I'm no better than all the people that got killed, you know, that were sent to Treblinka right away, you know. From your experiences during the war in, in concentration camps, is there anything that you learned that you want to pass on to your children, grandchildren, and future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say that the most important thing in the world is tolerance, you know. We hope that people learn to be tolerant of each other, regardless of the religion or color of skin or national-- or nationality. Because, like I say many times, just because you're a Jew, it doesn't mean that you're perfect. And just because somebody's not Jewish doesn't mean that they are bad. It's what the people are. There are plenty, plenty, plenty good people who are not Jews, good-hearted people, charitable people, just like there are some that are not. And there are many, unfortunately, Jews who are not so good, who can do things that are bad for other-- to other people. So basically, I would say the most important thing in the world is tolerance. And what I would also like to-- for personal achievement, I think that hard work and, and honesty and-- I would say also prudence, in other words. If you-- if you make some money, if you get ahead, it's not important how much you make, it's how much you have left, you know. Because it's very easy to make and spend. And we were very prudent in our life. There were times when we couldn't go on vacations. And we wouldn't go on-- we wouldn't go on vacations. We used to take the kids to the park. And that was their vacation. We used to play in the park.

speaker: interviewer
text: So early beginnings were very difficult in this country. They were very hard. But, like I said, we worked very hard. And we tried to save as much as we could.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a good social life because we belonged to the Jewish survivors-- different clubs where we met other people. And that was basically what kept us together, from drifting away. I think it was very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you for your effort. I appreciate it. OK, the picture that we see here, at the bottom left is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Abraham, or Abraham in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aleksander, of course. At the bottom right is my mother, bless her memory. Her name is Pola or [? Pesa ?] in Jewish. And standing in the middle am I, Joseph-- at that time was Josef Leib. In Polish, they used to call me Lolek. And then on the right, standing, is my brother Joel. In, in, in Warsaw, he was called 'Yo-el.' So he's-- here, he is called Joel. And he lives in Minneapolis now. He is one year and two months older than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, I would-- I would evaluate its-- it was taken around 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, Poland. It's a family picture. OK. Here we see a picture of my present-day wife, Johanna. And it was taken in Stuttgart, Germany, in 1945 or '46. And-- I don't know exactly. And at that time, she was 17 years old. And as you see, she was beautiful. That's why I fell in love with her-- one of the reasons. She also used to sing and dance and make everybody happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here we see my picture taken in Stuttgart, I think, around 1946. And at that time, I was 22 years old. So, like, what else can I say? [LAUGHS] I don't want to brag about myself. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is a picture that was taken at the wedding of my older son's-- Allan. And as we see here, on the left side is my youngest son Michael. And next to him is Allan with a mustache. And next to him is Sherry, his wife. Her maiden name was [? Shands. ?] Now, of course, it's Sherry Aleksander. Next to her, the tall lady, is Johanna, my wife. And on the right am I-- myself. So that was taken at the wedding about 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: --know, let's see, yeah. It was in the-- in the Stephen Wise Temple in Los Angeles, you know. OK, here we see a picture of my two grandsons, our two treasures, love of our life. On the left is Daniel, Daniel Paul is his middle name. And he's now 12 years old. And on the right, we see Derek. Derek Brice is his name. And he is now 10 years old. I think at the time that this picture was taken, they were about, I would say, seven and-- or eight and six, something like that. So the picture is a few years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when was it taken, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks-- it looks to me-- Derek looks to me like he's around six years old-- five or six. So it probably was taken about four years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in-- where they live, in, in Santa Barbara, California. That's where they live. My oldest son is a lawyer there. --I wouldn't know. OK. Here we see displayed-- on the right side, is my affidavit that I was in Stuttgart, Germany, at the United-- UNRRA camp and that I was employed as a camp policeman, which was mainly just to keep order in waiting lines and peace, you know, so far around the camp. And this is my document showing that I was employed there-- and signed Josef Aleksander. At that time was J-O-S-E-F Aleksander. Now it's J-O-S-E-P-H. OK. Here is displayed-- on the left side is a-- when we came to Stuttgart, we had to have some documents because we had no papers at all, no, no identification papers. So they issued us this-- these passes so that we could come-- go in and come back, you know, leave the camp and come back in without being stopped. And when we were stopped, we could identify ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- these were the only identification papers that we had. And it shows that it's a Displaced Person camp, and my name, Aleksander, Josef, and Stuttgart, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And below is the same type of pass-- identification paper issued to my wife. And it says Aleksander, Johanna, Stuttgart, Germany. And it's issued by the UNRRA Camp Committee. OK. This document here shows-- the, the, the upper document has-- is a diploma that I finished a course, a driving-school course, which was driving, basically, a, a, a truck. And it shows that I graduated from the course. I passed the course as a chauffeur.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the bottom document is another identification that shows that I was employed at the UNRRA camp. 30-- April 30th, 1945 or '6? Can't see-- '6.

speaker: interviewee
text: '6-- 1946, of course. In '45, I was still in concentration camp. [LAUGHS] I can't see from this side. OK, so this is basically just a piece of history that shows that I was there. And I worked. And I did my best. Thank you very much.
